title: Conjecture 1.2
statement_text: The expected number of faces in a random embedding of the complete graph $K_{n}$ is at most $2\ln n + O(1)$.
context_text: Computer simulations suggest that the upper bound of Theorem 1.1 (Stahl) is too high. Mauk and Stahl conjectured this tighter bound for the complete graph $K_n$. The paper's Theorem 1.8 establishes the correct logarithmic order $\Theta(\ln n)$ but leaves the precise constant open.
paper_contribution: Proves (Theorem 1.8) that the expected number of faces of $K_n$ satisfies $\mathbb{E}[F(K_n)] = \Theta(\ln n)$, confirming the logarithmic order but not yet the precise constant $2\ln n + O(1)$.
paper_title: Random Embeddings of Graphs: The Expected Number of Faces in Most Graphs is Logarithmic

title: Conjecture 1.13
statement_text: Let $p = p(n)$ be the probability of edges in $G(n,p)$. The expected number of faces in a random embedding of a random graph $G \in G(n,p)$ is $(1 + o(1))\ln(pn^{2})$.
context_text: In light of the paper's theorems for specific graph families and Monte Carlo experiments, the authors conjecture that a logarithmic bound holds for any usual model of random graphs. Extending the proof of Theorem 1.5 to arbitrary random graphs requires further ideas; Section 9 provides additional discussion.
paper_title: Random Embeddings of Graphs: The Expected Number of Faces in Most Graphs is Logarithmic

title: Conjecture 9.1
statement_text: For any $p(n) : \mathbb{N} \rightarrow [0,1]$, almost all graphs in $G(n,p)$ satisfy $\mathbb{E}[F] = O(\log(n))$.
context_text: Corollary 7.3 establishes a polylogarithmic bound for almost all dense graphs (when $p \geq 1/\mathrm{polylog}\, n$), and Section 8 gives a logarithmic bound for random sparse graphs. The authors conjecture that the logarithmic property holds without any density condition on edges. This conjecture would follow from the stronger Conjecture 1.13.
paper_title: Random Embeddings of Graphs: The Expected Number of Faces in Most Graphs is Logarithmic

title: Conjecture 9.2
statement_text: The expected number of faces in a random embedding of a random graph $G \in G(n,M)$ is $(1 + o(1))\ln(M)$.
context_text: Conjecture 1.13 is restated here in terms of the $G(n,M)$ model of random graphs with $n$ vertices and exactly $M$ edges. This follows naturally from Conjecture 9.1 and extends the logarithmic-face-count phenomenon to the fixed-edge-count random graph model.
paper_title: Random Embeddings of Graphs: The Expected Number of Faces in Most Graphs is Logarithmic

title: Conjecture 9.3
statement_text: Let $G$ be a graph on $n$ vertices with minimum vertex degree $\Omega(n)$. Then $G$ satisfies $\mathbb{E}[F] = \Theta(\log(n))$.
context_text: Families of sparse graphs with maximum degree $O(1)$ achieving $\mathbb{E}[F] = \Theta(n)$ are known, but no dense graph example with that many average faces has been found. Theorem 1.8 confirms this conjecture for the complete graph $K_n$; the general dense case remains open.
paper_title: Random Embeddings of Graphs: The Expected Number of Faces in Most Graphs is Logarithmic

title: Conjecture 9.4
statement_text: The expected number of faces in a random embedding of the complete graph $K_{n}$ is $2\ln n + O(1)$.
context_text: This is an explicit restatement of the Mauk–Stahl conjecture (cf.\ Conjecture 1.2) in Section 9, now in the stronger exact-asymptotic form suggesting the precise multiplicative constant. Theorem 1.8 confirms the $\Theta(\ln n)$ order but the constant $2$ remains unproved.
paper_contribution: Proves (Theorem 1.8) that $\mathbb{E}[F(K_n)] = \Theta(\ln n)$, confirming the logarithmic order; the exact constant $2\ln n + O(1)$ remains open.
notes: Stronger restatement of Conjecture 1.2 (upper bound only) in Section 9 as an exact asymptotic; both attributed to [36, page 289] (Mauk–Stahl 1996).
paper_title: Random Embeddings of Graphs: The Expected Number of Faces in Most Graphs is Logarithmic

title: Conjecture 9.5
statement_text: The expected number of faces in a non-orientable random embedding of the complete graph $K_{n}$ is at most $\ln(n) + O(1)$.
context_text: The authors extend their investigation to non-orientable surfaces, where a random embedding is defined by choosing a random rotation system together with a random signature assigning each edge a sign independently with probability $1/2$. Computer simulations support a logarithmic behaviour analogous to the orientable case.
paper_title: Random Embeddings of Graphs: The Expected Number of Faces in Most Graphs is Logarithmic

title: Conjecture 9.6
statement_text: Let $F^{-}$ be the random variable for the average number of faces in a non-orientable random embedding of some graph $G$. Then $\mathbb{E}[F^{-}] \leq \mathbb{E}[F]$.
context_text: The authors conjecture that the expected face count in a non-orientable random embedding is always at most that of the orientable random embedding, for any graph $G$. The inequality is verified for toy models (including the dipole and chains of triangles joined by cut edges), and an analysis of Random Process A gives the bound $\mathbb{E}[F^{-}] \leq \frac{1}{2}\mathbb{E}[F] + 1$ for the dipole.
paper_title: Random Embeddings of Graphs: The Expected Number of Faces in Most Graphs is Logarithmic
